critical_issues.title: Hardcoded default license signing secret in production code path
critical_issues.description: LICENSE_SIGNING_SECRET defaults to 'super_secret_signing_key_change_me' via os.getenv fallback. If the environment variable is unset, this weak, publicly-known secret is used to sign subscription licenses. An attacker can forge any subscription license.
critical_issues.recommendation: Remove the default value entirely. Fail hard at startup if LICENSE_SIGNING_SECRET is not set in production. Use os.environ['LICENSE_SIGNING_SECRET'] which raises KeyError if missing.

critical_issues.title: Duplicate hardcoded default signing secret in blockchain service
critical_issues.description: The same weak default 'super_secret_signing_key_change_me' is used for subscription signature verification in verify_subscription_on_chain(). This is a separate copy of the same vulnerability - if either location is used without the env var, the system is compromised.
critical_issues.recommendation: Centralize the signing secret to a single source. Remove all default fallback values. Fail at import time if the secret is not configured.

critical_issues.title: Subscription signing uses SHA256 instead of HMAC-SHA256
critical_issues.description: License signatures are computed as hashlib.sha256(f'{payload_str}{start_secret}'.encode()).hexdigest(). This is a simple hash concatenation, NOT HMAC. It is vulnerable to length-extension attacks and does not provide proper message authentication. The key is simply concatenated with the message rather than used in a proper MAC construction.
critical_issues.recommendation: Use hmac.new(secret_key, payload_str.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest() instead of plain SHA256.

critical_issues.title: Reference to undefined variable 'stellar_account_data' at runtime
critical_issues.description: The check_subscription_status endpoint calls blockchain_service.verify_subscription_on_chain(account_id, stellar_account_data) but stellar_account_data is never defined in the main.py scope. This will raise a NameError on every call, making the endpoint completely non-functional.
critical_issues.recommendation: Import stellar_account_data from app.services.blockchain or instantiate it locally before use.

critical_issues.title: Deterministic key derivation creates single point of failure for all users
critical_issues.description: All user Stellar keypairs are derived via SHA256(SECRET_KEY:UID). If SECRET_KEY is ever leaked (and it's used as the master derivation key), every single user's wallet key can be derived. The backend also has full knowledge of every user's secret key (derive_keypair returns the full keypair, and it's used in set_account_data/delete_account_data), making this a custodial system despite claims of being non-custodial.
critical_issues.recommendation: This architecture fundamentally contradicts the 'non-custodial' claim. The backend should NEVER have access to user secret keys. Consider: (1) HD wallet with per-user derivation paths and client-side signing, (2) server never touches secret keys, (3) use Pi SDK's built-in wallet instead.

critical_issues.title: KYB middleware allows unauthenticated requests through when token not in cache
critical_issues.description: When require_auth=True and the token is not found in the in-memory cache, the middleware executes 'pass' and continues to call_next(), allowing the request through without any authentication. The comment says 'route-level security handles heavy lifting', but this means ANY endpoint that relies solely on middleware for auth is completely unprotected. An attacker with a novel token bypasses all KYB checks.
critical_issues.recommendation: If require_auth is True and the token is not in cache, either: (1) perform full verification in middleware, or (2) reject the request with 401. Never silently pass through unauthenticated requests.

critical_issues.title: SSE notification stream has NO authentication - anyone can subscribe to any merchant's payment notifications
critical_issues.description: The /stream endpoint accepts merchant_id as a plain query parameter with no authentication or authorization. Any unauthenticated user can subscribe to a merchant's real-time payment notifications by guessing/enumerating merchant IDs, receiving all payment confirmation details including amounts and transaction hashes.
critical_issues.recommendation: Add authentication (verify_pi_token dependency) and verify that the authenticated user's UID matches the requested merchant_id.

critical_issues.title: get_data endpoint allows reading ANY account's blockchain data
critical_issues.description: The GET /data/{account_id}/{key} endpoint only requires the requester to be authenticated, but does NOT verify that the authenticated user owns the target account_id. Any authenticated Pi user can read any other merchant's stored data by simply providing their account_id. This leaks all on-chain data including invoices, business data, etc.
critical_issues.recommendation: Derive the account_id from the authenticated user's UID and reject requests where account_id doesn't match.

critical_issues.title: KYC status endpoint always returns 'completed: True' without actual verification
critical_issues.description: The /api/pi/kyc-status endpoint unconditionally returns {completed: True, status: 'verified'} as long as the Pi token is valid. The comment even admits 'Pi API doesn't explicitly expose KYC Status field'. This means unverified/non-KYC users are treated as fully verified, bypassing any KYC requirements.
critical_issues.recommendation: Check the Pi API response for actual KYC fields or user roles. Return 'unknown' or 'unchecked' if the API doesn't provide KYC status. Do not assume verification.

critical_issues.title: Imports from non-existent module 'app.api.v1.endpoints'
critical_issues.description: public_api.py imports from app.api.v1.endpoints (invoices, products) but this module does not exist in the source tree. This will cause an ImportError when the module is loaded, potentially crashing the entire application if it's included in the router tree.
critical_issues.recommendation: Either create the missing endpoints module or remove/comment out the import.

critical_issues.category: Security - Token Caching in Memory and Redis
critical_issues.title: Raw Pi access tokens stored in memory and Redis cache
critical_issues.description: verify_pi_access_token() caches the full user_data (including implicit token association via cache key f'pi_token:{token}') both in-memory and Redis. The in-memory token_cache dict stores tokens as keys with no eviction policy. This means: (1) tokens persist in memory for the lifetime of the process, (2) tokens are written to Redis where they may persist longer, (3) if the server or Redis is compromised, all cached tokens are exposed.
critical_issues.recommendation: Cache only non-sensitive identifiers (uid, username) with a short TTL. Clear in-memory cache entries when they expire. Consider using a session ID instead of caching the raw token.

high_issues.title: Content Security Policy allows 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval' for scripts
high_issues.description: The CSP header includes 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval' in script-src, which effectively defeats XSS protection. An attacker who can inject HTML can execute arbitrary JavaScript. Additionally, 6+ external domains are whitelisted (sdk.minepi.com, app-cdn.minepi.com, esm.sh, unpkg.com, cdn.jsdelivr.net) dramatically increasing the attack surface.
high_issues.recommendation: Use nonces or hashes for inline scripts. Remove 'unsafe-eval'. Minimize external domains to only absolutely required ones.

high_issues.title: Rate limiting completely bypassed by User-Agent spoofing
high_issues.description: The rate limiter skips ALL rate limiting checks if the User-Agent contains 'testclient' or 'pytest'. An attacker can trivially bypass rate limiting by setting User-Agent header to 'testclient' or including 'pytest' in any User-Agent string.
high_issues.recommendation: Remove the User-Agent bypass entirely, or gate it behind an environment check (e.g., only in development mode, verified by ENVIRONMENT setting, not by User-Agent).

high_issues.title: blockchain router registered under two different prefixes
high_issues.description: blockchain.router is included twice: once under '/api/blockchain' and once under '/api/pi'. This means all blockchain endpoints (including get-stellar-account, data CRUD) are accessible via two different URL paths, creating confusion and potential security policy inconsistencies.
high_issues.recommendation: If the '/api/pi' prefix is intentional for specific endpoints, create a separate router with only those endpoints. Do not duplicate the full blockchain router.

high_issues.title: Synchronous Stellar SDK calls block the async event loop
high_issues.description: StellarAccountData methods (set_account_data, get_account_data, delete_account_data, list_account_data) call synchronous Stellar SDK operations like server.load_account(), server.submit_transaction(), and server.accounts().account_id().call() directly within async functions. These are HTTP calls that will block the entire event loop for the duration of the request.
high_issues.recommendation: Run blocking Stellar SDK calls in a thread pool executor using asyncio.to_thread() or loop.run_in_executor().

high_issues.title: Unbounded in-memory dicts for transactions and invoices grow without limit
high_issues.description: pending_transactions, used_transactions, and registered_invoices are plain dicts that grow indefinitely. used_transactions has a 30-day cleanup but only runs when a new transaction is verified. registered_invoices has NO cleanup at all. In a long-running server, this will eventually exhaust memory.
high_issues.recommendation: Implement periodic cleanup tasks, use LRU caches with size limits, or move to Redis/TTL-based storage.

high_issues.title: Circular dependency between security.py and main.py via ProxyAwareIPExtractor
high_issues.description: create_session_token() and verify_pi_token() import ProxyAwareIPExtractor from app.main, but app/main.py imports from app.core.security at module level. This works due to lazy/deferred imports but is fragile and will break if import ordering changes. It also makes testing extremely difficult.
high_issues.recommendation: Extract ProxyAwareIPExtractor into its own utility module (e.g., app/core/utils.py) that has no dependencies on app.main.

high_issues.title: Bug report submission endpoint has NO authentication
high_issues.description: The POST /telemetry/logs endpoint accepts unauthenticated log submissions from anyone. An attacker can use this to: (1) flood logs with garbage (DoS), (2) inject fake entries into audit trail, (3) potentially inject misleading information into debugging logs.
high_issues.recommendation: Add authentication dependency or implement rate limiting and input validation at minimum.

high_issues.title: Python hash() used for IP anonymization is non-deterministic
high_issues.description: ip_hash uses str(hash(request.client.host)) but Python's hash() is randomized per process (PYTHONHASHSEED). The same IP produces different hashes across server restarts, making the hash useless for correlation, analytics, or rate limiting. Additionally, hash() can produce negative values.
high_issues.recommendation: Use hashlib.sha256(ip.encode()).hexdigest()[:16] for deterministic, consistent IP hashing.

high_issues.title: Internal exception details exposed in HTTP error responses
high_issues.description: Multiple endpoints in payments router use str(e) in HTTP response details (lines 96, 145). This can expose internal implementation details, file paths, API keys, or stack traces to attackers.
high_issues.recommendation: Return generic error messages to clients. Log full exception details server-side only.

high_issues.title: Blockchain write errors expose internal exception details to client
high_issues.description: The /data POST endpoint returns f'Blockchain write failed: {str(e)}' to the client, potentially exposing Stellar SDK error messages, connection details, or account information.
high_issues.recommendation: Return a generic 'Blockchain operation failed' message. Log the full exception server-side.

high_issues.title: CORS configured with wildcard methods and headers
high_issues.description: CORSMiddleware is configured with allow_methods=['*'] and allow_headers=['*']. While origins are restricted via settings, allowing all methods (including DELETE, PATCH, PUT) and all headers reduces the defense-in-depth provided by CORS.
high_issues.recommendation: Explicitly list only the methods needed (GET, POST, OPTIONS) and specific headers required.

high_issues.title: SECRET_KEY as short as 10 characters accepted with only a print warning
high_issues.description: The validator allows SECRET_KEY as short as 10 characters. For JWT signing and other cryptographic operations, 10 characters is trivially brute-forceable. The warning is only a print() which can be missed in production logs. Production mode should require at least 32 bytes of entropy.
high_issues.recommendation: In production environment, reject keys shorter than 32 characters with a hard error (ValueError), not just a warning.

high_issues.title: Pi Network access token stored inside JWT session payload
high_issues.description: The session JWT contains 'accessToken': access_token in its payload. JWT payloads are base64-encoded (not encrypted), so anyone with the JWT cookie can extract the Pi access token. If the JWT is intercepted (e.g., via XSS), the attacker gets both the session AND the underlying Pi token.
high_issues.recommendation: Do not store the raw Pi access token in the JWT. Store only a reference (session ID) and keep the token mapping server-side.

high_issues.title: New aiohttp/httpx ClientSession created for every API call
high_issues.description: Both _try_local_node/_try_public_api (aiohttp) and _call_pi_api (httpx) create a new client session for every single call. This prevents connection reuse, increases latency, and wastes resources (TCP handshake + TLS negotiation per request).
high_issues.recommendation: Create a single shared ClientSession at startup and reuse it for the lifetime of the application. Close it on shutdown.

high_issues.title: Role-based access control always returns OWNER role for all users
high_issues.description: _get_user_role() unconditionally returns Role.OWNER for every user. The CASHIER role and all permission checks are completely non-functional. The entire KYB middleware is effectively a no-op for authorization.
high_issues.recommendation: Implement actual role storage (database or on-chain) and role retrieval logic. Until then, the middleware gives a false sense of security.

medium_issues.title: Duplicate router imports at module level
medium_issues.description: auth, blockchain, telemetry, notifications are imported at line 30 AND again at line 460. The second import is redundant.
medium_issues.recommendation: Remove the duplicate import at line 460.

medium_issues.title: Bare except clause in purchase_subscription catches all exceptions
medium_issues.description: The except: clause at line 516 catches SystemExit, KeyboardInterrupt, and GeneratorExit in addition to actual errors. This makes it impossible to gracefully shut down the server while this code is running.
medium_issues.recommendation: Use 'except Exception:' instead of bare 'except:'.

medium_issues.title: RedirectResponse imported inside middleware on every request
medium_issues.description: The force_https middleware imports RedirectResponse from fastapi.responses on every non-HTTPS request in production. While Python caches module imports, this is still unnecessary overhead in a hot path.
medium_issues.recommendation: Move the import to the top of the file with other imports.

medium_issues.title: Subscription purchase does not actually verify the payment
medium_issues.description: The purchase_subscription endpoint comments say 'For now, we assume it's valid if we receive it'. The payment_id and txid are accepted without any verification against the Pi Network API. An attacker can generate fake payment_ids to get free subscriptions.
medium_issues.recommendation: Implement actual payment verification by calling the Pi API to confirm the payment exists and matches the expected amount.

medium_issues.title: datetime.utcnow() is deprecated since Python 3.12
medium_issues.description: create_session_token uses datetime.utcnow() which is deprecated. It will be removed in a future Python version.
medium_issues.recommendation: Replace with datetime.now(datetime.timezone.utc).

medium_issues.title: Cache decorator uses Python hash() for cache key generation
medium_issues.description: The cached() decorator generates keys using hash(str(args) + str(kwargs)). Python's hash() is non-deterministic across process restarts (PYTHONHASHSEED randomization). This means cached data from one process cannot be found by another process using Redis, defeating the purpose of distributed caching.
medium_issues.recommendation: Use hashlib.sha256 or a deterministic string representation for cache keys.

medium_issues.title: Redis URL read from os.getenv duplicates settings.REDIS_URL
medium_issues.description: CacheManager reads REDIS_URL from os.getenv('REDIS_URL') instead of using the centralized settings object. This means changes to settings won't be reflected, and the default value 'redis://localhost:6379/0' is hardcoded independently of config.py.
medium_issues.recommendation: Accept redis_url as a constructor parameter and pass settings.REDIS_URL from the caller.

medium_issues.title: Redis connection only supports redis:// scheme, not rediss:// (TLS)
medium_issues.description: The URL validation only checks for 'redis://' prefix. The 'rediss://' scheme for TLS-encrypted connections is rejected, forcing insecure connections to Redis.
medium_issues.recommendation: Accept both 'redis://' and 'rediss://' URL schemes.

medium_issues.title: get_data endpoint double-encodes values as base64
medium_issues.description: The value returned from stellar_data.get_account_data() is already a decoded UTF-8 string. The endpoint then base64-encodes it again. If the client expects base64, this is redundant; if the client expects raw data, this is wrong. The comment says 'Value is returned as base64 string (Stellar stores bytes)' but Stellar returns base64 which is already decoded by the service.
medium_issues.recommendation: Determine the expected format and apply encoding consistently. The service layer should handle encoding/decoding; the router should pass through.

medium_issues.title: active_connections dict has no size limit
medium_issues.description: The active_connections dictionary stores all SSE connection queues with no upper bound. An attacker could open thousands of connections to exhaust server memory.
medium_issues.recommendation: Implement a maximum connection count per merchant and globally. Reject new connections when the limit is reached.

medium_issues.title: Payment amount tolerance differs between payments router and blockchain service
medium_issues.description: The payments router uses a tolerance of 0.001 Pi (line 73) while the blockchain service uses 0.0000001 Pi (line 377). This inconsistency means payments that pass frontend validation could fail backend verification or vice versa.
medium_issues.recommendation: Define the tolerance as a single constant in a shared location and use it consistently.

medium_issues.title: PII detection only checks top-level string representation of metadata
medium_issues.description: The PII check converts the entire metadata dict to a string and checks for forbidden keys. This approach: (1) produces false positives if any value contains the word 'address' or 'key', (2) misses nested keys like {'user': {'email': 'x'}} because the check looks at the serialized string, not the actual keys.
medium_issues.recommendation: Recursively walk the metadata dict and check actual keys, similar to the AuditLogger._redact() approach.

medium_issues.title: Production JS files cached with 1-year immutable cache
medium_issues.description: JavaScript files in production get Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable. While this is efficient, there's no cache-busting mechanism (no version hash in URLs). If a JS file is updated, all users will continue serving the old version for up to a year.
medium_issues.recommendation: Implement cache-busting via content hashes in filenames (e.g., app.a1b2c3.js) or use query string versioning.

medium_issues.title: require_role decorator doesn't work with FastAPI dependency injection
medium_issues.description: The require_role() decorator wraps an async function but doesn't integrate with FastAPI's dependency injection system. When used on a route, it won't properly interact with other dependencies, request validation, or OpenAPI schema generation.
medium_issues.recommendation: Convert require_role into a FastAPI dependency function instead of a decorator.

medium_issues.category: Logic Error - Overly Aggressive Circuit Breaker
medium_issues.title: All 5xx responses trigger circuit breaker, including 500 for invalid payment_id
medium_issues.description: The _call_pi_api method treats all 5xx responses as circuit breaker failures. However, a 500 for /payments/{invalid_id} should not count against the circuit breaker - it's a client error (wrong payment_id), not a server infrastructure failure.
medium_issues.recommendation: Only trigger circuit breaker on infrastructure-level errors (502, 503, 504). Treat specific 500 responses based on the error context.

medium_issues.category: Performance - Memory Leak in Rate Limiter
medium_issues.title: Rate limiter clients dict grows without bound
medium_issues.description: The RateLimitMiddleware stores per-IP request timestamps in self.clients (defaultdict(list)). While entries within each list are cleaned (older than 60s), the dict itself grows as new IPs connect and is never pruned. Over time, this accumulates entries for every IP that ever connected.
medium_issues.recommendation: Periodically clean up IP entries that have no recent requests, or use an LRU cache with a size limit.

medium_issues.title: list_data returns empty dict on exception instead of propagating error
medium_issues.description: The GET /data endpoint catches all exceptions and returns {'entries': []} instead of indicating an error. The client cannot distinguish between 'no data found' and 'server error occurred'.
medium_issues.recommendation: Return appropriate error responses for different exception types.

medium_issues.title: os.makedirs('logs') called in both audit.py and main.py
medium_issues.description: Both modules call os.makedirs('logs', exist_ok=True) independently. While harmless due to exist_ok=True, it indicates poor initialization organization.
medium_issues.recommendation: Create a central initialization function that handles all filesystem setup.

medium_issues.title: StellarAccountData hardcoded to public Stellar network, not Pi testnet/mainnet
medium_issues.description: StellarAccountData uses 'Public Global Stellar Network ; September 2015' as the network passphrase and 'https://horizon.stellar.org' as the server URL. Pi Network uses its own Stellar fork with a different network passphrase and Horizon server.
medium_issues.recommendation: Configure the correct Pi Network Stellar horizon URL and network passphrase, likely from environment variables.

medium_issues.title: Bug report log_data logged directly without sanitization
medium_issues.description: The submit_bug_logs endpoint logs the raw log_data dict via json.dumps(log_data). Despite the PII check in the telemetry event endpoint, this endpoint has no PII filtering at all and logs whatever is sent.
medium_issues.recommendation: Apply the same PII filtering as the telemetry event endpoint, or at minimum the AuditLogger._redact() approach.

low_issues.title: import time inside multiple methods instead of module level
low_issues.description: import time appears inside _get_in_memory, _set_in_memory, and _cleanup_in_memory methods. While Python caches imports, this is a code quality issue.
low_issues.recommendation: Move 'import time' to the top of the file.

low_issues.title: getattr used for settings fields that always exist
low_issues.description: Lines 51-52 use getattr(settings, 'LOG_MAX_BYTES', 5*1024*1024) and getattr(settings, 'LOG_BACKUP_COUNT', 3) but these fields are defined in config.py with defaults. The fallbacks are unreachable.

low_issues.title: Mixed naming conventions: snake_case and camelCase
low_issues.description: The codebase mixes snake_case (Python convention) with camelCase from the Pi SDK (e.g., user_data.get('accessToken')). This is understandable for API interop but should be normalized internally.
low_issues.recommendation: Convert API response keys to snake_case immediately after receiving them.

low_issues.title: Block of 10+ comment lines in list_data endpoint
low_issues.description: The list_data endpoint has a large block of comments (lines 168-180) that appear to be the developer's internal monologue about data format. This should be resolved and the comments cleaned up.
low_issues.recommendation: Resolve the uncertainty about data format, then clean up the comments.

low_issues.title: Log message reveals internal file path
low_issues.description: The log rotation info message includes the log file path, which could reveal server directory structure to anyone with log access.
low_issues.recommendation: Use a generic log message without the full path.

low_issues.title: circuit_breaker_open attribute is redundant with CircuitBreaker.state
low_issues.description: BlockchainService.circuit_breaker_open (line 99) duplicates the information already available via self.pi_api_breaker.state. It's set in some places but not all, potentially becoming out of sync.
low_issues.recommendation: Remove circuit_breaker_open and use pi_api_breaker.state directly.

low_issues.title: Domain verification uses '*' in non-production, contradicting other CORS settings
low_issues.description: The pi_app_verification endpoint sets Access-Control-Allow-Origin: '*' in development. While the file is public by design, this inconsistency could confuse developers about CORS policy.
low_issues.recommendation: Use consistent origin handling across all endpoints.

low_issues.title: Duplicate numbered comment (#2. Approve)
low_issues.description: Comment at line 82 says '# 2. Approve' but the previous step was also '# 2. SECURITY: Verify'. Comment numbering is off.

low_issues.title: Version says '2030 Vision - Phase 4' but app version is 1.0.0
low_issues.description: The public API status returns phase '2030 Vision - Phase 4' which appears to be aspirational rather than actual. This is misleading for API consumers.

low_issues.title: Depends, HTTPException, and List are imported but never used
low_issues.description: from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException and from typing import List are imported but none are used in the file.

low_issues.title: Error response JSON built with f-string instead of json.dumps
low_issues.description: The 403 response uses an f-string to build JSON: f'{{"error": "Insufficient permissions..."}}'. This is fragile and could break if role names contain special characters.
low_issues.recommendation: Use JSONResponse(content={...}) or json.dumps() for proper JSON construction.

low_issues.title: httpx imported at module level in blockchain.py but also imported inside _call_pi_api
low_issues.description: httpx is not imported at the top of the file but is imported inside _call_pi_api method (line 517). Meanwhile, aiohttp is imported at the top. The import should be at module level for consistency.
low_issues.recommendation: Add 'import httpx' to the top-level imports.

summary: This codebase has severe security vulnerabilities that make it unsuitable for production use without major remediation. The most critical issues are: (1) a hardcoded default signing secret that allows license forgery, (2) weak SHA256-based subscription signing instead of HMAC, (3) an authentication bypass in KYB middleware that passes unauthenticated requests through, (4) an unauthenticated SSE notification endpoint exposing real-time payment data, (5) an authorization bypass in the blockchain data read endpoint allowing any authenticated user to read any merchant's data, (6) a completely non-functional KYC check that always returns 'verified', (7) a broken import in public_api.py that would crash the app, and (8) a reference to an undefined variable (stellar_account_data) in main.py. Architecture-wise, the code suffers from circular imports between security.py and main.py, blocking I/O in async contexts (Stellar SDK), no connection pooling for HTTP clients, unbounded in-memory caches, a deterministic key derivation scheme that contradicts 'non-custodial' claims (the backend can derive all user secret keys), and a non-functional RBAC system. The CSP allows 'unsafe-inline' and 'unsafe-eval', rate limiting is trivially bypassable via User-Agent spoofing, and Pi access tokens are cached in memory and Redis. The code quality is below average with duplicate imports, dead code, inconsistent error handling, excessive inline comments, bare except clauses, and misleading configuration. On the positive side, the code does implement some good patterns: privacy-aware logging, audit logging with PII redaction, circuit breaker pattern for API resilience, memo validation for Stellar, anti-replay transaction protection, and session JWT binding to IP/User-Agent. However, these positives are far outweighed by the critical vulnerabilities.
